preFix.title: Introducing a mated queen to a colony
preFix.subtitle: Getting a new queen accepted when requeening or rescuing a failing hive.
preFix.excerpt: Releasing a new queen straight into a colony almost always fails. Workers raised under the old queen treat a stranger as a threat. The introduction cage gives the new queen time to establish her pheromone while the colony eats through the candy plug. This tutorial covers when to requeen, how to prepare the colony, and the correct sequence for cage introduction.
preFix.sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), section on queen introduction and requeening. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
British Beekeepers Association (BBKA) technical data sheet on queen introduction methods.
Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), chapter on queen management, Internet Archive.

content.text: A colony will kill a new queen if she arrives without warning. Her
pheromone
profile is wrong, and workers that recognise her as a stranger will ball her. A
queen cell
left unsealed in a colony has the same problem: the colony must be queenless and receptive before any new queen survives introduction.

The cage method works by letting bees get used to the new queen's scent through the cage mesh while she eats toward them through a candy plug. By the time she emerges, the colony has absorbed her pheromone and accepts her.
When to requeen

Three situations call for queen introduction. The first is a dead or missing queen with no viable queen cells in the hive. The second is a failing queen whose laying pattern has become drone-dominated or patchy and you have a replacement ready. The third is a deliberate requeening for better temperament or productivity. The best time of year is late spring through mid-summer when there is a nectar flow on and the colony has young bees ready to accept a new queen.
Check for laying workers before you proceed
A colony that has been queenless for more than three weeks may have
laying workers

. Do not try to introduce a mated queen into a laying-worker colony. Signs are multiple eggs in worker cells and scattered drone brood across the comb. A laying-worker colony needs a frame of young open brood inserted first to reset the worker's ovary suppression, then introduction can proceed two weeks later.

attrs.body: Remove and kill the old queen at least two hours before placing the introduction cage. If you cage the new queen before removing the old one, the colony is no longer properly queenless and will be less receptive. Check every frame in the brood box; she is sometimes on the side walls of the box rather than on the comb.
attrs.title: Finding the old queen before introduction

content.text: What you need
Lit smoker with dry fuel
Full bee suit, gloves, and hive tool
Queen introduction cage with candy plug intact
Placing the cage
Light the
smoker

and suit up. Open the hive and give a few puffs of smoke under the crown board. Locate the brood combs and find a frame at the centre of the brood nest, where young nurse bees are most concentrated. These nurses are the most receptive to a new queen.
Hang the
propolis

-free introduction cage between two central frames, with the candy-plug end upward and the screen face in contact with the comb. The candy end must face upward so the queen's attendants can move clear of any dead attendants blocking the plug. Many beekeepers remove the attendants from the cage before fitting it, to reduce competition for the candy.
Close the hive without disturbing the cage. Do not open it for at least three days. Opening it early disturbs the introduction process.
Checking after three days

After three to four days, open the hive. If the candy has been eaten through and the cage is empty, the queen is out. Look for her on the surrounding combs. You are not trying to spot her immediately; you are checking the colony's mood. A calm hive with bees fanning at the entrance is usually a good sign. Do a full
inspection
five to seven days later to confirm the queen is laying.

items.fix: Remove the cage and confirm the old queen has been killed. If laying workers are present, insert a frame of young open brood and wait two weeks before trying again.
items.cause: Colony not yet queenless or has laying workers
items.symptom: Workers clustering aggressively on the cage mesh

items.fix: Gently pierce the candy with a match to open a small channel so the bees can eat through from both sides.
items.cause: Plug is too hard or cage is positioned poorly
items.symptom: Candy not eaten after five days and queen still inside

items.fix: Do a full inspection. If there is no brood and there are scattered drone cells in worker cells, the colony has laying workers. The introduction has failed; obtain a new queen and follow the laying-worker reset procedure before trying again.
items.cause: Queen killed after release, possibly a laying-worker colony rejecting her
items.symptom: Queen released but colony is still queenless after ten days

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
unflagged-jargon: "propolis" — domain jargon — register in glossaryTerms[] with a definition
